steps.name: onboarding
steps.review_criteria: As a security educator who makes complex topics accessible: Could a CTF beginner understand how to use this tool? Is there guidance on what to do first? Does the empty-graph progressive disclosure help or confuse newcomers? What would you add to make this a teaching tool? Would you feature this in a YouTube tutorial?

Are there any obvious UI elements that would mislead someone who's never used a binary analysis tool? Would you recommend this to a viewer who just finished their first CTF?

steps.script: (() => { const btn = document.querySelector('[data-testid="tab-final-site"]'); if (btn) { btn.click(); return 'clicked'; } return 'not found'; })()
(() => { const btns = document.querySelectorAll('button'); for (const b of btns) { if (b.textContent?.includes('Seed Namespaces')) { b.click(); return 'seeded'; } } return 'not found'; })()

(() => { const nodes = document.querySelectorAll('circle, [data-node-id]'); if (nodes.length > 0) { nodes[0].dispatchEvent(new MouseEvent('click', { bubbles: true })); return 'clicked node'; } return 'no nodes'; })()

(() => { const nodes = document.querySelectorAll('circle, [data-node-id]'); if (nodes.length > 0) { nodes[0].dispatchEvent(new MouseEvent('dblclick', { bubbles: true })); return 'double-clicked to expand'; } return 'no nodes'; })()

steps.name: click-to-expand
steps.review_criteria: As someone who teaches by showing: Is the click-to-expand pattern discoverable? Does single-click show detail and double-click expand? Is this convention explained anywhere? Would a beginner accidentally expand or not know they can expand? Is the expansion visually clear (new nodes appearing, animation)? Does it feel like exploring a map?

(() => { const btns = document.querySelectorAll('button'); for (const b of btns) { if (b.title === 'Code / Source view') { b.click(); return 'switched to code'; } } return 'button not found'; })()

(() => { const items = document.querySelectorAll('div, span'); for (const el of items) { if (el.textContent === 'droid' && el.offsetWidth > 0) { el.click(); return 'clicked'; } } return 'not found'; })()

(() => { const btns = document.querySelectorAll('button'); for (const b of btns) { if (b.textContent?.includes('Code') || b.textContent?.includes('</>')) { b.click(); return 'code'; } } return 'not found'; })()

steps.name: copy-paste-writeup
steps.review_criteria: As someone who includes code in CTF writeups and videos: Can you easily copy code snippets for a writeup? Does the copy include syntax highlighting metadata (for Markdown code blocks)? Can you copy with or without line numbers? Is the formatting blog-ready? Would you use this to prepare screenshots or code snippets for a YouTube video?

steps.name: nl-understanding
steps.review_criteria: As someone who explains things in plain English: Can the chat understand plain English questions about the binary? Could a beginner ask 'what does this program do?' and get a useful answer? Does it handle vague questions gracefully? Is the chat the most beginner-friendly entry point to analysis? Would you recommend starting with the chat instead of the graph?

steps.name: question-suggestions
steps.review_criteria: As someone who structures learning with good questions: Are the suggested questions educational? Do they teach you how to think about binary analysis? Are they ordered from simple to complex? Do they adapt to the current context? Would a beginner learn the tool's capabilities just by clicking through suggestions? Are they engaging enough to be clickbait-worthy?

steps.name: followup-quality
steps.review_criteria: As someone who builds understanding through Q&A: After clicking a suggested question, are follow-up questions offered? Do they go deeper into the topic? Is there a natural conversation flow? Can you build up understanding iteratively? Would this feel like talking to a knowledgeable mentor? Does the conversation get more technical as it progresses?

Is the error message helpful ('I don't know' vs 'Try asking about...')? Can you rephrase and try again easily? Does the chat suggest alternatives when it fails? Would a frustrated beginner give up or try again based on the error handling?

Can you click an entity in the chat to select it in the graph? Are entity names styled differently from regular text? Does this create a tangible connection between the conversation and the visual display? Would this help a beginner connect abstract concepts to concrete UI elements?

Is the tone appropriate for both beginners and experts? Are responses concise or do they dump too much information? Is there a conversation history? Would you enjoy using this as a primary interface for binary analysis?

steps.name: color-coding
steps.review_criteria: As someone who uses color to teach: Are the colors in the graph meaningful (different colors for different types)? Is there a legend explaining the color coding? Would a colorblind viewer understand the graph? Are the colors consistent throughout the tool (same type = same color everywhere)? Is the color scheme aesthetically pleasing for screenshots and videos?

steps.review_criteria: As someone who makes every visual element teach: Do the icons in the toolbar and views convey their meaning without labels? Are icon metaphors appropriate (magnifying glass for search, tree for hierarchy)? Would a newcomer understand the view-switching icons? Are icons consistent with common conventions? Would you understand every button without hovering for a tooltip?

steps.name: graph-readability
steps.review_criteria: As someone who makes screenshots and thumbnails: Is the graph readable at the current zoom level? Can you read node labels? Are edges clear at both zoomed-in and zoomed-out levels? Would a screenshot of this graph be understandable in a YouTube thumbnail? Is there a 'presentation mode' for making clean screenshots?

Are interactive elements (buttons, links) clearly distinguishable from static text? Would the UI look good in a YouTube video without color correction? Is the dark theme dark enough or too dark?

Would viewers complain about readability?

Would this replace OBS + screenshot tool for documentation?

steps.name: step-recording
steps.review_criteria: As someone who demonstrates analysis step-by-step: Does the tool record your analysis steps automatically? Can you replay them like a presentation? Could you share a recording with students? Would step recording enable 'analysis demos' where the graph builds up progressively? Is this better than recording a screen capture?

steps.name: click-intuitive
steps.review_criteria: As someone who values intuitive interactions: Does clicking a node do what you'd expect? Is single-click = select and double-click = expand obvious? Are click targets large enough to hit easily? Does clicking empty space deselect? Is there visual feedback on hover (cursor change, highlight)? Would a newcomer understand the click model without instruction?

steps.name: drag-behavior
steps.review_criteria: As someone who expects drag to work like a map: Can you drag nodes to rearrange them? Can you drag the background to pan? Is the distinction between drag-node and drag-pan clear? Does drag feel smooth and responsive? Would a mobile/touch user be able to interact? Is there inertia/momentum when panning?

steps.name: dblclick-expansion
steps.review_criteria: As someone who discovers features by clicking: Is the double-click expansion behavior discoverable? What happens — does it expand neighbors, zoom in, or open a detail view? Is the result what you'd expect? Is there an undo if the expansion clutters the graph? Would a beginner accidentally trigger this and get confused?

steps.script: (() => { const btn = document.querySelector('[data-testid="tab-final-site"]'); if (btn) { btn.click(); return 'clicked'; } return 'not found'; })()
(() => { const btns = document.querySelectorAll('button'); for (const b of btns) { if (b.textContent?.includes('Seed Namespaces')) { b.click(); return 'seeded'; } } return 'not found'; })()

(() => { const nodes = document.querySelectorAll('circle, [data-node-id]'); if (nodes.length > 0) { nodes[0].dispatchEvent(new MouseEvent('contextmenu', { bubbles: true, clientX: 200, clientY: 200 })); return 'right-clicked'; } return 'no nodes'; })()

steps.name: right-click
steps.review_criteria: As someone who expects context menus in professional tools: Does right-click open a useful context menu or the browser default? If custom, are the options understandable to a beginner? Are advanced options hidden or grouped? Is there a 'What can I do here?' option? Would a beginner learn the tool's capabilities from the right-click menu?

(() => { document.dispatchEvent(new KeyboardEvent('keydown', { key: 'Tab', bubbles: true })); return 'tabbed'; })()

steps.name: keyboard-nav
steps.review_criteria: As someone who advocates for inclusive tools: Can you navigate the tool using only the keyboard? Does Tab move through interactive elements in a logical order? Can you use arrow keys in the graph? Are keyboard shortcuts available? Is there a keyboard shortcut reference? Would a viewer with a broken mouse be able to use this tool?

steps.name: screen-reader
steps.review_criteria: As someone who wants tools to be accessible: Are there ARIA labels on interactive elements? Are graph nodes described with aria-label for screen readers? Is the graph structure conveyed through an accessible tree alternative? Are role attributes used correctly? While the graph is inherently visual, is there a text-based alternative representation?

Is the tab order logical (left-to-right, top-to-bottom)? Are focus traps avoided? Can you see where focus is at all times? Would the focus indicators be visible in the dark theme?

steps.name: colorblind-safety
steps.review_criteria: As someone who knows 8% of male viewers are colorblind: Does the graph rely solely on color to convey information? Are there shape, size, or pattern differences alongside color? Would a deuteranopic viewer miss critical information? Are the red/green color pairs avoided for important distinctions? Is there a colorblind mode? Would you add a disclaimer in a video about color-dependent features?

steps.script: (() => { const btn = document.querySelector('[data-testid="tab-final-site"]'); if (btn) { btn.click(); return 'clicked'; } return 'not found'; })()

steps.name: network-errors
steps.review_criteria: As someone who demos tools live and things break: What happens if the backend is unreachable? Is there a clear error message or does the UI just hang? Would a beginner understand what went wrong? Is there a retry button? Does the error state look professional or like an unhandled exception? Would this embarrass you in a live demo?

steps.script: (() => { const btn = document.querySelector('[data-testid="tab-final-site"]'); if (btn) { btn.click(); return 'clicked'; } return 'not found'; })()
(() => { const btns = document.querySelectorAll('button'); for (const b of btns) { if (b.textContent?.includes('Seed Namespaces')) { b.click(); return 'seeded'; } } return 'not found'; })()

(() => { const inputs = document.querySelectorAll('input[type=text], input[type=search], input:not([type])'); for (const i of inputs) { if (i.placeholder?.toLowerCase().includes('search') || i.placeholder?.toLowerCase().includes('filter')) { i.focus(); i.value = 'zzz_nonexistent_999'; i.dispatchEvent(new Event('input', { bubbles: true })); return 'searched nonsense'; } } return 'no search input'; })()

steps.name: empty-results
steps.review_criteria: As someone who encounters every edge case: When a search returns nothing, is the message helpful? Does it say 'no results' or does it say 'try X instead'? Is the empty state different from a loading state? Would a beginner think the tool is broken when they get no results? Is there a way to clear the search and try again that's obvious?

steps.name: loading-states
steps.review_criteria: As someone who demos tools on camera: Are loading states clearly indicated (spinner, skeleton, progress bar)? Is there a way to know how long to wait? Does the UI remain interactive during loading? Would a viewer watching a demo understand that data is loading vs the tool being broken? Are loading animations smooth and professional?
